content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Early selection at age 10–12 into parallel 6- and 8-year grammar schools is the primary structural cause of Hungary's current position as Europe's most socially selective education system and should be phased out by 2035 in favor of single-entry-age (14 or 15) and unified secondary-school admission to reduce inequality of opportunity without sacrificing demonstrable value-added in pedagogy.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Approximately 8–12% of a Hungarian age cohort enters 6- or 8-year grammar school (gimnazium) tracks, selected at age 10 or 12 largely via central written examination; entrants are strongly skewed toward high-SES, urban families.

Since 2022, Hungary has become Europe's most socially selective school system according to PISA; Hungary's social selectivity index worsened by 5.3 points between 2018 and 2022, the largest deterioration in Europe alongside Poland's tracking re-introduction.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: What is the true causal magnitude of peer effects vs. pedagogical quality in the observed 10% value-added in elite tracks? Current research separates them only weakly.
How much of the performance gap between grammar and vocational students would narrow if school choice and catchment sorting were held constant and only early selection were removed?
